provenance.source_corpus: Ecological Factors Affecting White Pine, Red Oak, Bitternut Hickory and Black Walnut Underplanting Success in a Northern Temperate Post-Agricultural Forest

source_spans.text: Tree shelters offering protection from deer browsing and species-specific site selection are recommended for underplanting in the southern Québec region.
Tree shelters offering protection from deer browsing and species-specific site selection are recommended for underplanting in the southern Québec region.
After 6 years, the survival and total height of unprotected oak was 29% and 44.3 cm vs. 80.5% and 138.5 cm for protected oak.
White pine was less affected by browsing (survival of 79.5 and 93.5%; height of 138.5 and 217.9 cm for unprotected vs. protected pine).
This study provides evidence that underplanting is suitable for black walnut assisted migration northward and for bitternut hickory restoration
This study provides evidence that underplanting is suitable for black walnut assisted migration northward and for bitternut hickory restoration
After 6 years, the survival and total height of unprotected oak was 29% and 44.3 cm vs. 80.5% and 138.5 cm for protected oak.
After 6 years, the survival and total height of unprotected oak was 29% and 44.3 cm vs. 80.5% and 138.5 cm for protected oak.
Height of all hardwoods was higher in the white ash stands
Height of white pine was higher in the grey birch stands
Total height of all hardwoods was significantly (p < 0.05) correlated with Rubus cover and with soil fertility.
Total height of all hardwoods was significantly (p < 0.05) correlated with Rubus cover and with soil fertility.
For white pine, significant positive relationships were observed between canopy openness or the basal area of grey birch and total height
For white pine, significant positive relationships were observed between canopy openness or the basal area of grey birch and total height
while soil CEC was found to be a significant negative predictor of total height
while total tree basal area, the basal area of grey birch and the cover of Gramineae species in the understory were negative predictors of walnut total height
while total tree basal area, the basal area of grey birch and the cover of Gramineae species in the understory were negative predictors of walnut total height
